recurring_phrases: this is the kind of stuff
lexical.notes: Heavy use of technical data/AI terminology (LLM, RAG, Unity Catalog, Delta Lake, Lakehouse, vector search, fine-tuned, instruction-following). Consistently uses product names and technical features. Mixes enterprise tech jargon with accessible enthusiasm markers.

typical_opener_patterns: Repost or endorsement of colleague/company content
typical_closer_patterns: URL link to blog or resource
Call to action (check it out, attend, watch)
structural.paragraph_shape: Short to medium paragraphs, typically 2-4 sentences. Often a single punchy opening statement followed by explanation and link.
structural.list_usage: Occasional numbered lists for feature breakdowns, but primarily prose-based announcements
structural.question_usage: Rare direct questions; posts are declarative and announcement-driven

rhetorical.attribution_style: Credits team broadly ('To the team that built this: THANK YOU'), names specific colleagues when resharing their content, references company milestones with specific numbers

examples: Exactly 10 years ago (Jan 2016), I stepped in as CEO. We had just closed out Q4 with a total of $600k in revenue (screenshot from board deck). Fast forward a decade. Q4 audited GAAP Revenue: $1,290M and over $5.4B revenue run-rate ending January. I've never tweeted our exact quarterly financials before, but seeing that $1.29B number cross my desk on my 10-year anniversary hit differently. To the team that built this: THANK YOU. If you're following the data space, you know exactly what this milestone means.

In just two weeks, we revolutionized Dolly. Introducing the first open source, instruction-following LLM, fine-tuned on a human-generated instruction dataset licensed for commercial use. With Dolly 2.0, any organization can create, own, and customize a powerful LLM without sharing their proprietary data with 3rd parties or paying for API access.

Thrilled to announce the GA of Row and Column Level Security in Unity Catalog! For the first time on any platform, you can apply fine-grained access controls across Apache Spark, data warehousing, AI, and real-time processing—all within a single platform. Unlock seamless, secure data collaboration without the complexity!

Power BI is just amazing. We've now GA'd a capability that lets you set up relationships in Unity Catalog—foreign keys, primary keys, schemas—and directly publish to Power BI with one button click (handles all credentials and Entra ID permissions). Your dashboards will always have the correct underlying data. Super slick!
This is the kind of stuff that's impossible to do with traditional databases, you need a Lakebase for this!
